segments.s094-g1: به marks the recipient/directional relation ('to' or 'toward'); the phrase به گستهم و طوس is a prepositional/dative phrase placed before the verb for emphasis. آمد is the simple past 'arrived/came' (3rd‑singular past) and the logical subject این آگهی follows the verb, a word order common in epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s094-g2: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of the news. تیره شد is a past construction: تیره (dark/ominous) + شد (became) = 'became dark/ominous.' آن فر شاهنشهی is the noun phrase 'that royal/imperial farr' (فر = royal glory); in this poetic clause the verb appears before its noun phrase object/subject.
segments.s094-g3: به here functions instrumentally ('by/with') introducing the means: به شمشیر تیز = 'with a sharp sword.' آن سر تاجدار is the object 'that head of the crown‑bearer/crowned one' (سر as 'head', تاجدار as 'crowned/one who bears the crown'); the modifiers follow the noun in this order typical of Persian epic diction.
segments.s094-g4: به زاری is an adverbial phrase 'in lamentation' (به used instrumentally/adverbially). بریدند is the past plural 'they cut' (3rd‑pl perfect/past). و links the clauses; برگشت کار is an elliptical, idiomatic phrase meaning 'the work/affair turned back' (برگشت here denotes a reversal/return and کار is the affected affair), with the copular/auxiliary sense implied rather than explicit.
segments.s094-g5: بکندند is a past plural verb 'they pulled/dug out' (here used for plucking hair). موی is the direct object 'hair' placed after the verb. The second verb شخودند is coordinated with the first (shared subject 'they') and takes روی ('face') as its object; both verbs describe ritual gestures of mourning. The exact meaning of شخودند is poetic/archaic (see senses).
segments.s094-g6: از ایران is a source phrase 'from Iran.' برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose/issued forth.' یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral 'a single/one' modifying های‌وهوی 'uproar/clamour'; the order is source → verb → noun phrase, a compact poetic sequence.
segments.s094-g7: سر here is 'head(s).' سرکشان is an epithet/agent noun ('the proud / rebellious ones') modifying or linked to سر (an older/genitive‑like compounding typical of epic diction). گشت is the past 'became/turned' and introduces the result phrases پرگرد و خاک: پرگرد 'to wheel about/whirl' and خاک 'dust' (the two give the image 'heads whirled and dust flew'). Word order places the brief verb before its pictorial predicates.
segments.s094-g8: همه دیده پر خون is an elliptical adjectival clause: همه ('every/everyone') + دیده ('eye') followed by the predicate پر خون ('full of blood'), where the copula is omitted (typical of terse poetic style). The second phrase, همه جامه چاک, mirrors this pattern: 'every garment (was) rent/has a gash.'
segments.s094-g25: This prepositional phrase begins the clause: به here marks means/with ('by/with'), followed by two coordinated nouns زاری و خواری ('lamentation and humiliation/abasement'). Then the direct object سرش را ('his head') appears: سر (head) + enclitic ش (his) + را (definite-object marker). Finally ز تن ('from the body') is a short prepositional phrase indicating separation. Word order is typical classical Persian: circumstantial/manner phrase → object (with possessor enclitic and object marker) → complement.
segments.s094-g26: بریدند is past-tense, 3rd-person plural, 'they cut / they cut off'. با introduces an accompanying or locational sense ('in/at/with'), here best read as 'in/at/with the famed assembly'; نامدار انجمن is a noun phrase (adjective + noun) meaning 'renowned assembly'. The verb precedes the phrase that situates the action, a common narrative order.
segments.s094-g27: همه is the plural subject 'all / everyone'. تیغ is the noun 'swords' and is followed by the descriptive adjective زهرآبگون ('poison‑green'), which here qualifies تیغ. برکشید is a past 3rd‑person plural verb 'they drew/they raised' (literally 'lifted up'), used transitively with تیغ understood as its object. The compact order places subject → noun phrase → verb for a brisk, poetic narration.
segments.s094-g28: This line contains an infinitival purpose/intent phrase followed by main-action verbs: به کین جستن ('to seek vengeance') is an infinitive construction with به marking goal/purpose and جستن the infinitive 'to seek'. آیید (here narrative 3pl 'they came/they advanced') follows, and the coordinated clause و دشمن کشید adds the action toward the enemy. In context کشید functions as a past verb 'they drew at/attacked/struck the enemy' (the exact verbal nuance is 'pulled/drew/attacked').
segments.s094-g29: همانا is an emphatic/exclamatory particle 'verily' or 'behold'. برین سوگ is an archaic compound: بر ('on/upon') + the demonstrative/archaic suffix ین (= این) giving 'upon this mourning' (سوگ = 'mourning'). با ما سپهر inverts the expected order: با ما ('with us') precedes سپهر ('the sky/heavens'), so سپهر is the subject understood as sharing the mournful state with 'us' — a poetically inverted order to emphasize 'with us'.
segments.s094-g30: This clause describes the physical sign of mourning. ز ('from') + دیده ('the eye/eyes') gives the source. فرو باردی is an older/poetic verb phrase meaning 'falls down' (فرو = downward; باردی is an archaic/poetic verbal form of باریدن/باردَن); the verb is placed before its nominal theme ('خون' = 'blood'), so 'blood falls down' reads verb → subject. به مهر ('because of/for love/affection') gives the cause or motive and follows the verb phrase as a circumstantial complement.
segments.s094-g31: شما نیز is the addressed subject 'you also'. دیده پر از خون is a descriptive NP: دیده ('eye/eyes') modified by پر ('full (of)') + از خون ('of blood'), so 'eyes full of blood'. کنید is the 2nd‑person plural/formal present or imperative-like form of کردن, here meaning 'make' or 'do' — a hortatory instruction: 'make your eyes full of blood' (i.e., shed blood/weep).
segments.s094-g32: همه opens the clause 'everyone / all'. جامه ناز is a noun phrase meaning 'garments of finery/delicacy' (جامه = garment; ناز qualifies with the sense of finery/delicacy), and بیرون کنید is a verbal phrase 'take out / remove' formed from بیرون + کنید (do/make), functioning here as an imperative/hortative 'cast off' or 'remove (your) fine clothes'. The order noun → modifier → verb is straightforward Persian: subject → object phrase → verbal command.
segments.s094-g41: رکابست is the fused/poetic form meaning ‘(the) stirrup (is)’. جایگاه is the noun ‘place’ and پای مرا (پای + ـم = ‘my foot’) is its genitive/possessive phrase; the clause reads literally ‘the stirrup is the place of my foot’. The enclitic ـم marks possession (‘my’) and را here is the object/possessive linking marker forming پایِ مرا.
segments.s094-g42: یکی = ‘one/a single’; ترگ تیره = a tattered/dark rag (ترگ ‘rag’, تیره ‘dark/tawny’) and سرم = ‘my head’ (سر + ـم). را marks the definite object/associates the rag with the head; کلاه = ‘cap/hat’. The whole segment states appositionally that a single dark rag serves as the speaker’s cap.
segments.s094-g43: بر + ین (orthographic demonstrative) = برین ‘because of this/over this’ (archaic/poetic formation). کینه is the noun ‘grudge/hatred’; آرامش و خواب نیست is the negative predicate ‘there is no tranquillity nor sleep’. The verb نیست negates the existence/possession of rest and sleep.
segments.s094-g44: همی is an adverb here meaning ‘still/always’; چون = ‘like/as’. دو چشمم = ‘my two eyes’ (چشم + ـم ‘my’); به جوی آب = ‘to/at a stream of water’ (به + جوی + آب). The final نیست negates the locative notion — literally ‘my two eyes are not at/into a stream of water’ — an idiomatic, poetic way of saying the eyes are not soothed/at rest.
segments.s094-g45: روان = ‘soul/spirit’ (the theme of the blessing); چنان = ‘such’ (demonstrative/qualifier) modifying the following title شهریار جهان = ‘sovereign/king of the world’. The phrase is an apposition: ‘the soul of such a king of the world’.
segments.s094-g46: درخشنده = ‘shining, radiant’; بادا is the optative/exhortative particle ‘may it be’ (a conventional wish in classical Persian). میان مهان = ‘among the guests’ (میان = among / middle of; مهان = guests), completing the blessing: ‘may (that soul) be radiant among the guests.’
segments.s094-g47: شما را addresses ‘you’ with را marking the addressee/beneficiary (common in blessings). به داد = ‘by/through the justice’ (به used as agentive/causal here) and جهان‌آفرین = ‘the world‑creator’ (epithet for God) provides the source of that justice. The phrase sets the cause/ground of the following wish.
segments.s094-g48: دل = ‘heart’ (subject of the wish); ارمیده = participial/adjectival ‘reposed/at peace’; بادا = optative ‘may it be’. به آیین و دین = ‘according to ritual/custom and religion’ (به marks the standard or manner). The whole segment is the wish: ‘may your heart be at rest, in accordance with custom and religion.’
segments.s094-g89: بزرگان ایران is a genitive phrase 'the great ones of Iran' (noun + country name). ز گفتار اوی is a prepositional/causal phrase 'because/of his speech' where ز (poetic for از) introduces the source or cause and اوی is the old/poetic possessive 'his.' Word order places the modifier (ز گفتار اوی) after the head noun phrase.
segments.s094-g90: بروی زمین برنهادند روی is a verbal clause: برنهادند (they raised/put) is the past-tense main verb and روی (face) is its object; بروی زمین is a locative phrase 'toward/on the ground' modifying the motion/position. The order (locative + verb + object) reflects poetic inversion but reads as 'they turned/placed their faces toward the ground.'
segments.s094-g91: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' از آفرینش بپرداختند uses از + noun to mark the domain/point they left or finished ('from creation') and بپرداختند is a past plural verb meaning here 'attended to/finished or passed from' (poetic sense of completing or moving on from an action).
segments.s094-g92: نوندی ز ساری برون تاختند is the main clause after the temporal clause. نوندی (a poetic/musical term) is the theme introduced, ز ساری 'out of/from the flowing/continuous (strain)' shows origin, برون is the adverb 'forth/out,' and تاختند (past plural) is the dynamic verb 'they rushed forth/burst out.' The chain gives 'a Navand burst forth from the flowing (tune).'
segments.s094-g93: بپویید is a past/plural verbal form meaning here 'they went/approached' (ب‑ prefix with the verb root gives a narrative past/habitual shade). نزدیک دستان سام is a locative/goal phrase 'near the hands/presence of Sam' where دستان (literally 'hands') names the court/side and سام is the proper name; the whole phrase is the destination of بپویید.
segments.s094-g94: بیاورد (he/they) brought is a simple past transitive verb; ازان is a poetic form 'from those/therefrom' (here 'from those'), نامداران is the source noun 'the famed/renowned ones' and پیام is the direct object 'message/tidings.' Word order is verb + source phrase + object, typical in poetry where subject may be implied or displaced.
segments.s094-g95: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'that' (or 'who'). بخشود is the simple past 'he forgave/granted'; بر ما uses بر as the dative/benefactive preposition 'to/for us,' and جهاندار ما ('our world-ruler/sovereign') is the subject placed after the verb by poetic inversion, so the clause reads 'that our sovereign pardoned/granted to us.'
segments.s094-g96: شد is the simple past 'became'; اغریرث is a poetic/metaphorical noun (a floral or figurative name) functioning as a predicative term; پر خرد is an adjectival phrase 'full of wisdom' (پر = 'full (of)') modifying either the predicative or the following noun; یار ما 'our companion/ally' closes the clause in apposition so the sense is 'our companion became (like) an aghrirath, full of wisdom.' Word order (verb – predicate – modifiers – possessor) is characteristic of the poetic diction.
segments.s094-g57: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase placing the scene 'in the world' (preposition + noun). به گفتار تو is another prepositional phrase meaning 'by your speech/word' (به used adverbially: 'by'). زنده‌ایم is a present‑tense, 1st‑person plural predicate 'we are alive / we live' that closes the clause; the possessive pronoun تو follows گفتار in the normal poetic order (noun + personal pronoun).
segments.s094-g58: همه یک به یک is an adverbial phrase 'all, one by one' (یک به یک = 'one by one', with به functioning adverbially). مر ترا is an older/pronounced clustering of pronouns and markers that yields the sense 'to you / yours' in this context (see note below). بنده‌ایم is the 1st‑person plural present 'we are servants' (بنده used as 'servant'/'humble self‑reference'). The whole phrase means 'we are all, one by one, your servants.'
segments.s094-g59: تو دانی که is the main clause + complementizer 'you know that...'. دستان is a substantive here (a group/name or metonymy: 'the Dastans' / 'those hands/men') functioning as the subject, and به زابلستان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in Zabulistan' (به marking location). The whole subordinate clause functions as the content of 'you know'.
segments.s094-g60: به جایست is a verbless/predicative locative construction: جایست (it 'lies' / 'is placed') with به marking location or state. با شاه کابلستان ('with the king of Kabulistan') is an accompanying/associative phrase specifying with whom they are — با = 'with'. Together this says they are located/placed together with the king of Kabulistan.
segments.s094-g61: چو (as/like) introduces a comparison or example. The sequence lists names (برزین, قارن) and the postpositive adjective/epithet رزم‌زن ('war‑fighter, warrior') attaches to them — a common poetic order where the descriptor follows the proper names. The clause is nominal/appositive rather than a full verbal sentence.
segments.s094-g62: Again چو introduces another pair of examples (خراد و کشواد). لشکرشکن is a compound epithet 'army‑breaker' that follows the names and describes their martial prowess. The construction is parallel to the previous segment: names followed by a descriptive nominal phrase.
segments.s094-g63: یلانند is a present, 3rd‑person plural predicative form meaning 'they are heroes/valiant men' (ylān as heroic figures). با + noun (با چنگهای دراز) is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with long claws' (چنگ here = 'claw/talon' used metaphorically for powerful hands/arms); دراز 'long' modifies چنگهای (the plural with possessive/construct implied).
segments.s094-g64: ندارند is the 3rd‑person plural negative present 'they do not have'. از ایران ('from / with regard to Iran') marks the domain or counterpart in relation to which the lack is claimed. چنین دست باز is a noun phrase 'such a free/open hand' (چنین = 'such', دست = 'hand' used idiomatically as 'power/room to act', باز = 'open/free'), so the full clause says they lack such freedom vis‑à‑vis Iran.
segments.s094-g65: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. تابند is a 3rd-person plural present form meaning 'they turn/are turning' (poetic word order places the verb before its noun). گردان is the noun 'host/contingent' (the subject). ازین سو is an adverbial phrase 'from this side' (از + این written as ین + سو). عنان ('reins') is the object/thing associated with their maneuver.
segments.s094-g66: به چشم اندر آرند is a verbal phrase 'they bring into (their) eyes' where به...اندر = 'into/ toward the eye' (اندر is archaic/poetic for در). آرند is 3rd-person plural 'they bring/put', and نوک سنان ('the tips of spears') is the direct object placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s094-g67: ازان is anadverbial 'thereupon/from that'. تیز گرد‌د is a copular construction 'becomes swift' (گردد = becomes); رد here means 'passage/charge' (the movement of forces) and افراسیاب names the agent/owner of that charge — word order is inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s094-g68: دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic -ش). گردد is 'becomes/turns'. از بستگان is a prepositional phrase indicating cause/concern 'about/regarding his relatives' and پرشتاب is a predicate adjective 'hurried/quick', describing the state his heart takes on.
segments.s094-g69: پس آنگه are consecutive temporal adverbs ('then'/'and then') introducing what follows. سر یک رمه بی‌گناه is a genitival noun phrase 'the head of an innocent flock' (سر + modifier 'یک رمه' follows the head in this poetic ordering, with بی‌گناه qualifying the flock).
segments.s094-g70: به خاک اندر آرد is the verbal clause 'he brings/puts (it) into the earth' (به ... اندر = 'into the dust/ground', آرد = 'he brings/puts'). ز بهر کلاه is a purposive phrase 'for the sake of a hat' (ز بهر = 'for the sake of'), placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s094-g71: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. بیند is 3rd-person singular present 'he sees'. اغریرث is the proper name (Aghrirath) and هوشمند is a postpositive epithet 'the wise', a common poetic order (name + epithet).
segments.s094-g72: مر here functions as an indirect object 'for me' (archaic/dialectal clitic). این بستگان را is the definite/object phrase 'these relatives' (را marks the definite direct object). گشاید is 3rd-person singular 'he loosens/frees', and ز بند is a prepositional complement 'from the fetter(s)/bond(s)'.
segments.s094-g17: سرت (your head) is the noun phrase and functions as the topic/subject; افسر (helmet) follows it as the thing sought. جوید is the classical present-tense verb 'seeks/searches' and همی is the old Persian progressive particle meaning 'still/continually'. The prepositional phrase از خاک ('from/out of the earth') indicates the source of what is sought, so the literal order is 'your head the helmet from earth seeks continually.'
segments.s094-g18: زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'; خون شاهان ('the blood of kings') is a genitive phrase that describes what the earth emits or is full of. ببوید is the classical present of بوییدن used in the sense 'to emit a smell' (the earth gives off the odor), and همی again marks continuous aspect. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic rhythm, but syntactically it's 'the earth smells (of) the blood of kings.'
segments.s094-g19: گیایی 'plants/vegetation' begins a relative clause introduced by که ('that/which'). روید is the classical present of روییدن used here in the sense 'grow / show face,' and بران بوم (بر + ان/آن contracted to بران + بوم) mean 'on that soil/land.' The coordinating و ('and') plus بر prepares the continuation in the next segment; overall this segment sets up 'plants which grow on that ground and on ...'.
segments.s094-g20: نگون here carries the sense 'downcast/overthrown' (poetically 'bent down'), and دارد is the present-tense verb 'has' / 'keeps' that links the adjective to the object; سر ('head') is the thing kept low. از شرم خورشید is an ablative phrase 'from shame of the sun' giving the cause. Together: '(they) have/keep the head bent down from the sun's shame.'
segments.s094-g21: همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle. داد ('justice') is the noun object placed before the verb; خواهیم is the first-person-plural present/future of خواستن used here as 'we will seek/demand.' The coordinated phrase و زاری کنیم uses کنیم (subjunctive/imperative-form of کردن) with زاری ('lamentation') to mean 'and we will lament.' The clauses use compound verb constructions common in classical Persian.
segments.s094-g22: به introduces the object/cause 'for/over' (here: 'over the blood of'), خون پدر is 'the father's blood,' and سوگواری کنيم is the verb phrase 'we will mourn' (سوگواری as noun 'mourning' + کنیم to form a verbal idea). The whole segment means 'we will mourn over the father's blood.'
segments.s094-g23: نشان فریدون is the noun phrase 'the sign/token of Fereydun.' بدو is the dative/locative 'in/on it' (referring back to a place), and زنده بود is the past-tense copula 'was alive/was still present.' The clause literally reads 'Fereydun's sign in it alive was,' i.e., the mark of Fereydun still existed there.
segments.s094-g24: زمین is 'the earth/ground' as subject; نعل اسپ ('the horse's horseshoe') is the thing mentioned, ورا ('to it' / 'on it') points back to the ground, and بنده بود is a classical poetic phrase meaning 'was fastened/attached' (here used adjectivally/participially). The sense is 'the horse's shoe was fixed to the earth there.' Note: بنده in this context is an archaic/poetic use meaning 'fastened/attached,' not the common sense 'servant.'
segments.s094-g137: پس از is a fixed sequence marker: پس (here 'after') + از ('from') form the prepositional phrase 'after'. The noun phrase that follows, نامور نوذر شهریار, is ordered as adjective (نامور, 'renowned') + proper name (نوذر) with the title شهریار ('king') in apposition after the name — a classical Persian pattern where titles often follow names.
segments.s094-g138: به سر خاک بر کرد is a compact classical phrase: به ('toward/onto') + سر ('head') + خاک ('dust/ground'), then the compound verb بر کرد (بر + کرد) meaning 'turned/laid upon' or 'cast (his head) onto'. The conjunction و links this to بگریست ('he wept' — past tense). زار is an adjective/adverb 'miserably/bitterly' modifying the verb 'wept'. Note 'بر کردن' here is an idiomatic/poetic past construction using کرد as the light verb.
segments.s094-g139: به شهر اندر آوردشان is a motion clause: به شهر ('into/to the city') with the older locative اندر ('in/within') reinforcing 'into', آوردشان is آورد + شان (bring them; شان = 3rd-person plural object enclitic). ارجمند follows as an adjective meaning 'honored/esteemed' applied to those brought; in classical Persian adjectives can follow or stand alone as a predicate.
segments.s094-g140: بیاراست here is a past transitive verb 'he adorned/arranged'. It takes the following noun phrase ایوانهای بلند ('the tall/lofty porticoes') as its object. Note the adjective بلند follows the noun, a normal order in Persian poetry, and ایوانهای is the plural form ('iwans/porticoes').
segments.s094-g141: چنان هم که is an idiomatic connector 'just as / even as'. هم adds emphasis ('even/also'). هنگام نوذر is a temporal phrase 'in the time/age of Nowzar'. بدند is an archaic/past third-person plural form (classical Persian) of بودن meaning 'they were' or 'they had the state of' describing how things were then.
segments.s094-g142: که introduces the subordinate descriptive clause. The repeated با before each item ('با تاج و با تخت و افسر') is a rhetorical, enumerative use of با ('with') listing regalia; افسر in classical usage means 'helmet' (not 'officer'). The clause ends with بدند again ('they were/they had'), repeating the past-state verb to describe possession or ornamentation.
segments.s094-g143: بیاراست again is a past verb 'adorned/arranged'. دستان (literally 'the hands') here functions idiomatically as agents ('the hands' i.e., those who labored) and همه دستگاه ('all the apparatus/arrangements') is the object. Classical word order and compact expression make the phrase concise; note that دستان can be agentive rather than literal.
segments.s094-g144: شد is the past auxiliary 'became' leading into the result phrase. از + خواسته ('from wish/desire') with بی‌نیاز ('free of need') yields 'became free from desire/need'. آن سپاه ('that army') is the final noun phrase modified by the preceding predicate. The construction is resultative: 'became free of desire that army.'
segments.s094-g9: سوی زابلستان نهادند روی is an instance of the idiom روی نهادن (to set/turn one's face toward). سوی زابلستان is a prepositional phrase 'toward Zabulistan' and نهادند is the 3rd person plural past tense 'they set/placed/turned'. روی is the noun 'face' used as the thing they 'set' (i.e. 'they turned their face').
segments.s094-g10: This is a coordinated nominal phrase describing manner or accompaniment: زبان شاه‌گوی و روان شاه‌جوی = 'the tongue (speech) of the king‑speaker and the spirit/soul of the king‑seeker.' زبان and روان are parallel nouns joined by و; each is in construct relation to the following epithet (شاه‌گوی / شاه‌جوی).
segments.s094-g11: بر + proper name is used as a directional preposition 'to/toward Zāl' (بر زال). رفتند is 3rd person plural past 'they went'. با سوگ و درد is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with mourning and sorrow', modifying how they went.
segments.s094-g12: رخان پر ز خون و سران پر ز گرد is two parallel verbless clauses describing appearance. X پر ز Y = 'X full of Y' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'from/because of'). Here (poetically) their faces/cheeks were full of blood and their heads full of dust; و links the two halves.
segments.s094-g13: که introduces reported speech/exclamation here. زارا functions as an interjective/emo­tive particle 'alas' or 'woefully' used in exclamation. دلیرا and شها are vocatives ('O brave one', 'O king!') and نوذرا is the proper name Nowzar in vocative position.
segments.s094-g14: گوا here works as a discourse particle meaning 'they said/it is said' (introducing what follows). تاجدارا is the plural/accusative form 'the crown‑bearers' (those who wear crowns). مها مهترا is a nominal phrase, here an epithet or name 'Great Mahtara' (مها = 'great', مهترا used as an epithet/name), the thing called out to the crown‑bearers.
segments.s094-g15: نگهبان ایران is a genitive phrase 'guardian/protector of Iran' (نگهبان + ایران). و شاه جهان is a coordinated title 'and king of the world' (شاه جهان = 'king of the world'); the two phrases function as appositive titles.
segments.s094-g16: سر تاجداران و پشت مهان are two parallel nominal phrases giving additional titles/attributes. سر here means 'chief/head' (سر تاجداران = 'head/chief of the crown‑bearers'), و links it to پشت مهان where پشت means 'support/protection' (پشت مهان = 'support/protector of guests').
segments.s094-g129: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آمد is the simple past 'came' (i.e. 'reached'). به دستان سام is a prepositional phrase 'into the hands of Sam' placed before the noun آگهی for poetic word order; آگهی is the thing that 'came' (the news/information).
segments.s094-g130: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. برگشت is past 'returned' (predicate of the clause). گشواد here is a proper name (Gushvad) not listed among the dictionary senses, so it refers to a person who returned. با فرهی is an adverbial phrase 'with honour/with nobility' describing the manner of return.
segments.s094-g131: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (someone); گنج ویژه is the direct object 'a special treasure' (noun + adjective). به درویش داد is the verb phrase 'gave to the dervish' with داد as the past tense 'gave' and the beneficiary marked by به.
segments.s094-g132: سراینده 'the poet' is marked by the object/topic marker را; جامه خویش is 'his garment' where خویش (here split as خوی + ش) functions possessively ('his own'), and داد again is the past 'gave' — together 'he gave his garment to the poet.'
segments.s094-g133: چو 'when' marks a temporal clause; گشواد here is a proper name (Gushvad) and thus not one of the listed verbal senses; نزدیک زابل رسید is the verbal phrase 'arrived/ reached near Zabol' with رسید in the simple past.
segments.s094-g134: پذیره شدش is a passive/causative expression meaning 'he was received' (شدش = 'it befell him/was effected for him' with the clitic ش = 'him'); زال is the subject who performs the welcoming; زر is the object 'gold'; چون سزید is a clause 'as he deserved/ as was fitting' (چون = 'as', سزید = past of 'to deserve' or 'to be fitting').
segments.s094-g135: بران 'thereupon' links to the previous action; بستگان 'the relatives/kin' is the subject. زار here functions as an adverbial/verb-like modifier 'lamentingly' (from زاریدن), and بگریست is past 'wept' or 'wailed' (poetic past form, here referring to the relatives). دیر 'long' specifies duration ('for a long time').
segments.s094-g136: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; مانده بودند is the past perfect/simple past compound 'had remained/been left' (مانده = remained; بودند = past auxiliary 'were'); در چنگ شیر is the prepositional phrase 'in the claw/grasp of the lion' giving the place/agent of captivity implied by the question.
segments.s094-g73: پراگنده is an adjectival/participial notion meaning “scattered/wide” placed before the verb. گردیم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive (–یم) of گردیدن/گشتن here meaning “let us become / let us be” (hortative). گرد جهان is an adverbial phrase meaning “around the world” where گرد functions as ‘around/about’ and precedes the noun جهان.
segments.s094-g74: زبان is the noun “tongue/speech” (direct object or theme). برگشاییم is first-person-plural hortative/subjunctive (–یم) of برگشودن/برگشايی used poetically = “let us open/reveal” (here “let us speak”). پیش مهان is an adverbial prepositional phrase “in the presence of / before the guests (or nobles)”; the verb at the end governs the whole clause.
segments.s094-g75: به پیش بزرگان is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the presence of / before the great ones”; پیش is the core meaning “before/in the presence of,” here headed by the preposition به. ستایش کنیم is the noun + verb construction where ستایش (“praise”) combines with کنيم (first-person-plural subjunctive/hortative of کردن) = “let us praise/offer praise.”
segments.s094-g76: همان here works adverbially as “the same/likewise” linking this clause to the previous. پیش یزدان = “before God” (پیش = in the presence of; یزدان = God in epic/Zoroastrian register). نیایش کنیم repeats the noun + کنيم pattern = “let us offer prayer,” parallel to the previous clause.
segments.s094-g77: چنین گفت is a formulaic “thus said” (چنین = thus/so, گفت here functions as the verb ‘said’). اغریرث is the proper name in apposition to the verb, followed by the epithet پرخرد (postpositive adjective) meaning “very wise.” Note the verb-before-subject order common in narrative poetry. (Lexicon for گفت given did not list the finite ‘said’ sense.)
segments.s094-g78: کزین = از + این contracted, “from this / out of this.” گونه گفتار = “this kind/manner of speech” (گونه modifies گفتار). کی درخورد is an interrogative phrase: کی here = “who” (rhetorical), and درخورد is an adjective meaning “worthy/suitable”; together “who would deem it fitting?” The clause is an evaluative rhetorical question about the appropriateness of the speech.
segments.s094-g79: ز من is a poetic prepositional phrase “on account of me / against me” (ز = from/because of). آشکارا شود is a verb phrase where آشکارا = “openly/evidently” and شود is 3rd-singular present/future subjunctive of شدن = “will become / will be manifested.” دشمنی is the abstract noun “enmity/hostility” that the verb predicates.
segments.s094-g80: بجوشد is 3rd-singular optative/predictive of جوشیدن with the prefix ب- = “will/shall seethe/gush,” used metaphorically for boiling anger. سر here is best read as “inner head/secret/heart” (inner thought or seat of feeling) and مرد = “man” (the person whose head/heart boils). آهرمنی is an adjective meaning “Ahrimanic/demonic,” so the phrase means “the (inner) head/heart of the demonic man will seethe.” Word order places the verb first for emphasis, with the noun phrase following.
segments.s094-g81: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'a certain / some' (i.e., 'another') modifying چاره. چاره 'remedy/plan' is the direct object of سازم. سازم is first-person singular present (classical usage with future sense) from ساختن: 'I make / I will make'. دگرگونه 'different' qualifies the plan. زین is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from this' (literally 'from this'), placed after the adjective.
segments.s094-g82: که introduces a subordinate/purposive clause here ('so that / in order that'). با من is a prepositional phrase 'toward/against me' (with با + pronoun). نگردد is negative present/subjunctive (نـ + گردد) meaning 'does not become / does not turn' and expresses prevention/wish. برادر is the noun that would 'become/turn' (subject in inverted order after the verb). به is the preposition 'into/towards' and کین is the noun 'hatred/vengeance' describing the state to be avoided.
segments.s094-g83: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون (literary) means 'it comes to pass / there comes' and sets up the condition. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. دستان here is a proper name in context (not the common noun 'hands'); it functions as the subject of شود. شود is third-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن 'become'. تیزچنگ is an epithet/name (here a personal name or epithet meaning 'Sharp-claw') and is the complement after شود.
segments.s094-g84: یکی is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one' as subject. لشکر 'army/host' is the thing brought. آرد is third-person singular present 'brings' (آوردن). بر ما is the prepositional phrase 'against us' (بر + pronoun). به جنگ is a goal/purpose phrase 'to battle' (toward battle). Word order is subject — object — verb/phrases typical of classical Persian flexibility.
segments.s094-g85: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آرد is 'he brings' (third-person singular of آوردن). به نزدیک is the prepositional phrase 'near to' (به + نزدیک). ساری here is an attributive adjective ('flowing, moving' in classical diction) modifying رمه; together ساری رمه yields 'the moving/flocking host' or 'the throng'. The phrase means 'when he brings the throng/host close.'
segments.s094-g86: به دستان is a dative/goal phrase 'into Dastan's hands' or literally 'to the hands' (here indicating the person Dastan or hands as recipient). سپارم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of سپردن 'I will entrust / I will hand over'. شما را is the marked direct object ('you' with the object marker را). همه 'all' follows for emphasis: 'you all.' The verb precedes the object for emphasis, common in poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s094-g87: بپردازم is an archaic/classical verb form here meaning roughly 'I will go / I will turn (off)' or 'I will depart' (the exact shade is classical-poetic). It is first-person singular. آمل is the place-name 'Amol' and acts as the destination. نیایم is negative present/subjunctive 'I will not come' (نـ + آیَم), and به جنگ 'to the battle' gives the action/location being refused. The pair contrasts leaving for Amol with not joining the fight.
segments.s094-g88: سرم را is the direct object 'my head' with the object marker را. ز is the poetic genitive/preposition 'from' (زِ). نام means 'name' and here 'reputation/fame'. اندرآرم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of اندرآوردن 'I put/bring into' (archaic); together ز نام اندرآرم 'I will take (my head) from fame' — i.e., remove my fame. به ننگ 'into shame/disgrace' expresses the resulting state. The clause is idiomatic: to 'bring one's head from fame to shame' = to exchange renown for disgrace.
segments.s094-g33: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that' introducing the judgement clause; با کین is a prepositional phrase 'with/ in vengeance' (با + noun), and شاهان is the plural subject 'kings'. نشاید is an archaic impersonal/predicative present meaning 'it is not fitting / unbecoming' and governs the following subordinate clause, here resumed by the second که introducing 'چشم...'.
segments.s094-g34: نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form (here part of the subordinate predication introduced above), used to state what is unbecoming; the predicate 'پر از آب' follows (پر = 'full (of)' + از = 'of'), and و coordinates the parallel predicate 'دل پر ز خشم' where پر ز (ز = poetic variant of از) means 'full of'. Word order is typical poetic/classical Persian: verb/predicate material can follow the noun it predicates.
segments.s094-g35: همه انجمن is the subject phrase 'the whole assembly' (همه = 'all, everyone' modifying انجمن). زار و گریان شدند is a predicative coordinate: زار (here 'lamenting/wailing') and گریـان ('weeping') are adjectival/participial predicates, with شدند (past plural of شدن) 'became' as the copular/auxiliary marking the past result.
segments.s094-g36: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بر + آتش is the prepositional phrase 'on/upon the fire'; تیز is an adjectival qualifier 'keen/fierce' modifying the fire (poetic order permits adjective after noun), and بریان شدند is the compound of the participle بریان ('roasted/consumed') with شدند 'became/were', i.e. 'were roasted' (passive/resultant sense).
segments.s094-g37: زبان داد دستان is an instance of classical inversion: دستان is the agent 'the hands' and زبان داد literally 'gave tongue' = 'gave voice / spoke' (داد is the past verb 'gave' used idiomatically). که introduces the content clause; تا is the marker 'until', and رستخیز is the noun 'Resurrection' (or uprising) serving as the temporal limit.
segments.s094-g38: نبیند is negative + present-subjunctive/optative ('may it not see' / 'shall not see') and expresses a vow/curse here. نیام is 'scabbard/sheath' and مرا (archaic ordering) marks possession ('my') placed before the object marker را; را is the definite/object marker attached before تیغ تیز, where تیغ تیز = 'sharp sword'. The whole clause reads as an optative: 'may my sharp sword not see its sheath.'
segments.s094-g39: چمان is a demonstrative/adverb 'such' or 'thus'. چرمه is 'leather/hide' (the noun), and در زیر تخت is the locative phrase 'under/beneath the throne' (در + زیر + تخت). منست is an archaic/persianized copular/predicative 'is fitting/appropriate' (from منس), so the clause states that such a leather beneath the throne is appropriate.
segments.s094-g40: سنان‌دار is a compound adjective 'spear-point-bearing / barbed' placed before نیزه 'spear'. درخت here means 'wood / timber' (material of the shaft), and منست again is the predicative 'is fitting/ appropriate' — together: 'The spear (with pointed head) has a shaft of wood / is made of wood.'
segments.s094-g97: This is a compact past-clause: 'یکی' (one/a single) modifies 'پیمان' (covenant/pledge); 'سخت' is an adjective meaning 'strong/severe' qualifying the pledge. 'فگندیم' is an archaic past plural (ماضیِ جمعِ اول/ما) of فکندن ('to cast' / 'to make'), so literally 'we cast/made'; 'بن' (foundation/base) follows as the thing established. The whole string means we established a single, firm pledge as a basis.
segments.s094-g98: 'بران' is a deictic adverb ('upon that/thereon') pointing back to the pledge; 'برنهادیم' is another past plural verb (برنهاد + -یم) meaning 'we placed/laid upon' or 'we set up'. 'یکسر' is an adverb meaning 'entirely/at once' and modifies 'سخن' (speech/word). The phrase therefore says they set that pledge up and then spoke a single, plain utterance.
segments.s094-g99: 'کز' here introduces origin/condition ('from' / 'if from'); 'ایران' is the place. 'چو' functions as a temporal/conditional 'when/if' or 'such as'; it links to the following verb. 'دستان' is used poetically for men/agents (literally 'hands' in the lexicon) and is modified by 'آزادمرد' ('free-hearted' / 'noble man'), so the noun phrase means 'brave/noble men.' This whole clause is a subordinate temporal/conditional clause leading into the verbs that follow.
segments.s094-g100: 'بیایند' is a present/jussive plural form meaning 'they may come' / 'they come' (classical Persian often uses this form to express a wished-for or potential action). 'و' simply coordinates. 'جویند' (here a verb from جستن/جستن + ی) means 'they seek' or 'they search for' — with 'نبرد' (battle) as its object; 'با وی' means 'with him.' Grammatically this is a coordinated verb clause expressing 'may they come and seek battle with him.'
segments.s094-g101: 'گرانمایه' is an epithet meaning 'precious/esteemed' that precedes the proper name 'اغریرث' (Aghrirath). 'نیک‌پی' is a descriptive epithet ('of good lineage' / 'well-born') attached to the name; together they form a nominal phrase 'the noble/esteemed Aghrirath, well-born.'
segments.s094-g102: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'آمل' is the place of origin. 'گذارد' here is a past-tense third-person form of گذاردن/'گذاشتن' (he set/moved/placed), so 'he moved/placed'; 'سپه' (army) is the direct object and is marked by the postposition 'را'. 'به' introduces the destination ('to') and 'ری' is the destination (Ray). The verb therefore narrates that he moved the army from Amol to Ray.
segments.s094-g103: 'مگر' introduces a negative-exceptive/conditional sense here ('unless'); 'زنده' means 'alive'; 'از' is the preposition 'from/out of'; 'چنگ' in this context is the physical image 'claw/grip' (not the musical instrument); 'این' is the demonstrative 'this' and 'اژدها' is 'dragon.' Together this clause means 'unless alive from the claw/grip of this dragon.'
segments.s094-g104: This noun phrase + verb states the consequence: 'تن' ('body') here stands for 'person(s)/the body of' and combines with 'یک جهان مردم' ('a whole world of people') to mean 'the body/entirety of a world's people.' 'آید' is a present/factual verb 'comes' (classical Persian use), and 'رها' is an adjective/participial idea meaning 'free/loosed.' The order is slightly poetic: the noun phrase precedes the verb and the adjective follows, yielding 'the bodies of a whole world's people come free/alive.'
segments.s094-g105: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. پوینده is the subject (a seeker/scout) placed before the verbal predicate. در زابلستان is a prepositional locative phrase ('in Zabulistan') and رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached' appearing at the end, the normal Persian verb position in poetry.
segments.s094-g106: سراینده is 'the bard/poet' as subject; در + پیش (= در پیش) marks location 'in the presence of' or 'before'. دستان literally 'hands' follows پیش; رسید is the same past verb 'arrived'. The clause thus reads literally 'the bard arrived in the presence (lit. before the hands)', a poetic way to say he came before the leaders.
segments.s094-g107: بزرگان و جنگ‌آوران is the coordinated object 'the elders and the warriors'. را is the definite-object marker placed after the coordinated noun phrase. بخواند here is a past/action verb meaning 'he called/summoned' (poetic past form), with the object preceding the verb as is common in verse.
segments.s094-g108: پیام یلان is a genitive phrase 'the message/tidings of the heroes'. پیش ایشان is a locative/adverbial phrase 'before them / in their presence'. براند is the past form (from راندن) meaning 'he caused to be brought / he set before' — an older/poetic past used here to say he brought or presented the heroes' message to them.
segments.s094-g109: ازان پس ('after that') and چنین ('thus') form an adverbial frame 'after that thus'. گفت normally is the past-tense verb 'he said' (here introducing speech); the provided sense list does not include this basic meaning, so no matching sense index is available. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and سروران is the vocative noun 'lords' addressed.
segments.s094-g110: This is a string of epithets in direct address: پلنگان جنگی ('war‑panthers' — metaphorical for fierce fighters) and نام‌آوران ('renowned ones'). In poetry such epithets stand in apposition to the preceding vocative (سروران).
segments.s094-g111: کدامست is a contracted classical form of کدام است 'which is (there)?' introducing a rhetorical question. مردی is 'a man' (indefinite). کنارنگ دل is an adjectival phrase modifying مردی; کنارنگ is an archaic/poetic adjective (here meaning something like 'turning aside' or 'fickle'), a sense not represented in the supplied options, and دل is the noun 'heart'.
segments.s094-g112: به introduces the indirect object/recipient 'to/for' (i.e., 'give his heart to a man'). مردی is the man who would receive it. سیه کرده is a past-participial verbal adjective meaning 'having made (it) black' or 'blackened' (سیه = black + کرده = made/done) and modifies that man; در جنگ is an adverbial 'in battle', and the final دل is the object 'heart' that has been blackened. Grammatically, سیه کرده functions as a participial modifier ('who has blackened [the] heart') and دل is the patient of that action.
segments.s094-g49: ز مادر همه مرگ را زاده‌ایم is literal, poetically compressed Persian. ز (preposition) + مادر (noun) puts an origin/source first: “from the mother.” همه modifies the implied human subject (we/all of us). مرگ را is marked with the object/definite marker را to single out Death, and زاده‌ایم is the perfect/passive sense ‘we have been born’ (first-person plural). The line therefore says we all come from (are born to/from) the mother called Death.
segments.s094-g50: برینیم و گردن ورا داده‌ایم joins two coordinated actions. برینیم is an archaic/poetic first‑person‑plural verbal form meaning something like ‘we bowed/bent’ (here meaning submission); it is not the regular modern verb form and functions as a past/perfective action. و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” گردن (direct noun) is the body part “neck,” ورا (vora) = ‘to it/unto her’ (the dative/directional clitic + pronoun referring back to Death), and داده‌ایم is ‘we have given’ (perfect, first‑person plural). Together: ‘we bowed and gave our necks to it/her.’
segments.s094-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” گردان is the subject “troop/troops.” سوی کینه is a directional phrase: سوی = ‘toward’ and کینه = ‘hatred/vengeance’ (here meaning an attack or hostile intent). بشتافتند is the simple past, third‑person plural of بشتافتن, ‘they hastened/ran.’ The clause thus means “When the troops hurried toward (their) enmity/vengeance.”
segments.s094-g52: به introduces a locative or directional phrase (“to/at”). ساری here functions as a place-name or adverbial locator (Sari/a place or ‘there’ in the poem’s geography/poetics). سران is the plural noun “leaders, chiefs,” and آگهی is “news/information.” یافتند is past, third‑person plural ‘they found/learned.’ Grammatically the clause says the leaders at (or in) Sari came to have/receive news (i.e. ‘the leaders learned the news’); the preposed به ساری gives the place or context.
segments.s094-g53: ازیشان = از + ایشان, literally ‘from them’ or ‘of them’ and indicates loss or change affecting them. بشد is an auxiliary/preterite particle meaning ‘it happened/it came to pass’ (narrative past), and خورد here is used in the sense ‘befell/was taken’ (not the literal ‘to eat’). The coordinated nouns آرام و خواب (‘rest and sleep’) are what is affected. Together the clause conveys that rest/sleep were taken away from them (i.e. they lost calm and repose).
segments.s094-g54: پر از بیم گشتند is a straightforward predicate: پر از = ‘full of,’ بیم = ‘fear,’ and گشتند is past, third‑person plural of گشتن ‘they became.’ The trailing از افراسیاب gives the cause/agent ‘because of/from Afrasiab.’ So: ‘they became full of fear on account of Afrasiab.’
segments.s094-g55: ازان پس is a common classical sequence meaning ‘after that’ or ‘thereupon’ (ازان = ‘from that/thereupon,’ پس = ‘after/then’). به اغریرث = ‘to Aghrirath’ (recipient of the message). آمد is past ‘came,’ and پیام is the noun ‘message/tidings’ (here the subject that ‘came’). Word order is poetic inversion: the message came to Aghrirath.
segments.s094-g56: که‌ای begins the quoted address; که here introduces the verbal content (‘that’ / ‘saying’) and ای is the vocative particle ‘O.’ پرمنش is an epithet ‘high‑minded’ or ‘noble in temper,’ مهتر is a title ‘chief/elder/distinguished man,’ and نیک‌نام is an epithet ‘well‑named’ i.e. ‘of good repute.’ Grammatically this is the opening of direct speech addressing Aghrirath with honorific epithets.
segments.s094-g121: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از پیش + noun is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of before/in the presence of'; here پیش carries the sense 'in the presence of' and precedes دستان. برون شد is a compound verbal idea 'went out, came forth' (برون 'out' + شد, past), and سپاه (army/host) appears after the verb as the noun that performed the action in this poetic word order.
segments.s094-g122: خبر شد is an impersonal/semipassive idiom meaning 'it became known' or 'word spread'. به marks the indirect object/recipient (to); اغریرث is a proper name here and نیک‌خواه is an appositive epithet 'the well-wisher' (adjective-noun compound used as a title).
segments.s094-g123: همه بستگان را is a definite object ('all the relatives') marked by را. به + ساری here gives a location or locus ('at/into Sari'); بماند (3rd‑person past/subjunctive-shaped form of ماندن) means 'he left/caused to remain' or simply 'remained' depending on context — the clause says he caused/left all his kin to stay at Sari. The subject is omitted (third person) and understood from the surrounding narrative.
segments.s094-g124: بزد is the past of زدن 'to strike/blow' and here governs نای (the instrument) — 'he blew the ney' (in epic diction a metal or war trumpet). رویین is an adjective 'iron/metal' modifying نای. The conjunction و links the next clause: لشکر براند uses براند (from راندن) 'to drive/put in motion' and here means 'he set the army moving' or 'drove the host on'; the combination mixes narrative past with vivid present-tense forms typical of epic style.
segments.s094-g125: چو = 'when'. گشواد here functions as a proper name in the narrative (although the listed senses are verbal; context shows a personal name) and فرخ is an epithet 'fortunate' attached to that name. به ساری رسید is a standard verbal phrase: به + place + رسید (past of رسیدن) 'reached/arrived at Sari'.
segments.s094-g126: پدید آمد is a two-word past verbal expression meaning 'appeared' or 'came into view' (پدید 'apparent' + آمد 'came'). آن بندها را is the definite object 'those bonds/fetters' marked by را, and کلید is the noun 'key' that appears; word order places the verb first and the appearing thing (کلید) at the end as the focal subject.
segments.s094-g127: This clause means 'he made/provided one horse for each (man)'. یکی اسپ is 'one horse' (یکی = one/a certain), مر is an enclitic/dative-like element here (poetic or dialectal) indicating beneficiary ('for'), هر یکی را emphasises 'for each one' with را marking the object of the benefaction, and بساخت is the past action 'he made/provided' (the expected reading in context); some listed lemma senses do not match the actual verbal reading, so the verb is treated as the past of ساختن/بساختن.
segments.s094-g128: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ساری here is a place (Sari) and سوی زابلستان 'toward Zabulistan' gives the destination; بتاخت (past of تاختن with a b- prefix common in epic narrative) means 'he galloped/charged off'. The phrase therefore describes a swift departure from Sari toward Zabulistan.
segments.s094-g113: خریدار is a nominative noun meaning 'one who takes/seeks' (here 'one eager for'); the phrase خریدار این جنگ و این تاختن lists two objects of that eagerness (این جنگ = 'this battle', این تاختن = 'this charge/charge of cavalry'). Word order is noun + object(s), typical Persian poetic compression: the noun 'خریدار' governs the following noun phrases.
segments.s094-g114: به introduces the goal or direction here ('toward/to'); خورشید گردن برافراختن is an infinitival/nominal phrase meaning 'to raise (one's) neck to the sun.' گردن is the direct object of برافراختن (to raise/erect). The whole phrase describes the intended proud or fatal gesture (raising one's neck toward the sun).
segments.s094-g115: ببر زد is a simple past clause: ببر (tiger) + زد (past of زدن) = 'the tiger struck' (subject + past verb). بران کار is a short adverbial phrase 'on that matter / in that affair' (بران = 'on/thereupon', کار = 'deed/affair'). کشواد دست places the proper name کشواد (Keshvad) before دست ('hand'), a possessive/affected noun: 'Keshvad's hand' or 'Keshvad laid (his) hand' depending on syntactic connection. Word order (verb earlier then adverbial then noun phrase) is typical of epic compression.
segments.s094-g116: This line is compressed and partly discontinuous: منم ('I too' / 'I also') precedes a reported utterance and subsequent action. گفت here is the verb 'said' (but the provided sense list gave noun senses; grammatically it functions as the past of گفتن). یازان is an imperative/infinitival root meaning 'open/widen' (an order or exclamation). بدین is orthographically split as ب + دین in the annotation but functions together as the adverb/pronoun 'to/with this' ('بدین' = 'by this / thus'). داد دست is a past-tense clause 'gave (his) hand' (داد = 'gave', دست = 'hand'), i.e. 'he handed over his hand / gave his help.' The line strings a speech act ('I too said
segments.s094-g117: برو here functions as an interjection/imperative ('go' or a shout of encouragement) that is the object of آفرین کرد ('uttered praise'). آفرین کرد is a compound verb: آفرین (acclamation/praise) + کرد (past of کردن = 'made/uttered'), and فرخنده زال follows as the agent ('Farokhandeh Zal [did this]'). Word order (interjection + verb + agent) is common in poetry where the exclamation is fronted and the subject comes after the verb.
segments.s094-g118: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'wishing that'); خرم is an adjective 'joyful/fortunate' describing the wished-for state. بدی here functions as an archaic verbal form meaning 'you give' / 'you grant' (a second-person past/present hortative sense in epic usage) rather than the noun 'evil' (the provided lexicon entries do not list this verbal use, so it is an irregular/archaic reading). تا بود ماه و سال is a temporal extension 'as long as moon and year exist' (تا = 'until/as long as', بود = past/optative 'be/exist', ماه و سال = 'months and years' / 'moon and year' as poetic trope for time). The clause is therefore a blessing/wish: 'that you be joyful as long as months and years last.'
segments.s094-g119: سپاهی is a collective noun 'army/force' or 'soldiers'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing origin; گردان is 'division/troop' and functions as a genitival modifier ('troops/regiments'); پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' qualifying گردان. The sequence 'سپاهی ز گردان پرخاشجوی' is therefore 'a force from aggressive regiments' (literally 'army from troop aggressive'), reflecting Persian noun-modifier order in compressed epic style.
segments.s094-g120: ز زابل به آمل نهادند روی is a full verbal clause: ز زابل ('from Zabul') sets origin, به آمل ('toward Amol') sets destination, نهادند is the past tense 3rd-person plural of نهادن ('they set/placed'), and روی (here) is 'face' used in the idiom رو نهادن به = 'to set one's face toward' = 'to turn toward/advance on.' So the verb phrase literally means 'they set their face toward Amol' — i.e., they advanced toward Amol. The verb is plural agreeing with سپاهی earlier.

sentences.s094-s1: This news reached Gostaham and Tus: that the royal glory of that imperial house had grown dark.
sentences.s094-s2: With a keen sword they cut off that crown‑bearer's head amid lamentation, and the course of events was reversed.
sentences.s094-s3: They plucked out hair and (beat/struck) their faces; from Iran there rose a single great clamour.
sentences.s094-s4: The proud ones' heads began to whirl and dust swirled; every eye was full of blood, every garment torn.
sentences.s094-s13: With lamentation and abasement they cut off his head from his body in the famed assembly.
sentences.s094-s14: All drew their poison-green swords; they came seeking vengeance and fell upon the foe.
sentences.s094-s15: Truly — upon this mourning the heavens are with us; from the eyes blood falls down for love.
sentences.s094-s16: You too, make your eyes full of blood; everyone, cast off the garments of finery.

sentences.s094-s22: Because of this grudge there is no rest or sleep; likewise, my two eyes are not at a stream of water.
sentences.s094-s23: May the soul of such a sovereign of the world be radiant among the guests.
sentences.s094-s24: May your heart be at rest by the justice of the Creator of the world, according to custom and religion.
sentences.s094-s45: The great men of Iran, at his words, turned their faces toward the ground.

sentences.s094-s29: By your word we live in the world; we are all, one by one, your servants.
sentences.s094-s30: You know that the Dastans are in Zabulistan; they are (situated) with the king of Kabulistan.
sentences.s094-s31: As Borzin and as Qarn, warriors; as Khraad and Keshvad, breakers of armies.
sentences.s094-s32: They are heroes with long claws; they do not have such a free hand with respect to Iran.
sentences.s094-s33: When the revolving host turns from this side, they bring the reins to their eyes and the spear-points into view.
sentences.s094-s34: Thereupon Afrasiab's charge becomes swift; his heart grows hurried about his kinsmen.
sentences.s094-s35: Then he lays the head of an innocent flock to the earth — for the sake of a hat.
sentences.s094-s36: If the wise Aghrirath sees (it), he will free these relatives for me from their bonds.
sentences.s094-s9: Your head still seeks a helmet from the dust; the earth still smells of the blood of kings.
sentences.s094-s10: Plants that grow on that land and on it keep their heads bent down from shame of the sun.
sentences.s094-s11: We will demand justice and lament; we will mourn our father's blood.
sentences.s094-s12: Fereydun's sign remained alive in it; the horseshoe of the steed was fastened to the ground there.
sentences.s094-s69: After that, the renowned king Nowzar bowed his head to the ground and wept bitterly.
sentences.s094-s70: He brought them into the city, honored them, and adorned the lofty porticoes.
sentences.s094-s71: Just as in Nowzar's day they were, who had crown and throne and helmet.
sentences.s094-s72: All the arrangements were set in order, and that army became free of want.
sentences.s094-s5: They turned their faces toward Zabulistan, with the tongue of the king‑speaker and the soul of the king‑seeker.
sentences.s094-s6: They went to Zāl in mourning and sorrow; their faces were full of blood and their heads full of dust.
sentences.s094-s7: They cried aloud: 'Alas! O brave one! O King Nowzar!' and, it is said, called to the crown‑bearers 'Great Mahtara.'
sentences.s094-s8: Guardian of Iran and king of the world, the head (chief) of the crown‑bearers and the support (protector) of guests.
sentences.s094-s65: When the news reached Sam's hands that Gushvad had returned in honour.
sentences.s094-s66: A certain man gave a special treasure to the dervish; he gave his own garment to the poet.
sentences.s094-s67: When Gushvad came near Zabol, Zal received him and, as was fitting, gave him gold.
sentences.s094-s68: Thereupon his kin bewailed him bitterly for a long time; where had they been left — in the lion's grasp?
sentences.s094-s37: Let us spread out across the world; let us open our tongues before the guests.
sentences.s094-s38: Let us offer praise before the great ones; likewise let us offer prayer before God.
sentences.s094-s39: “Thus said the very wise Aghrirath: ‘From such words who would find them fitting?’”
sentences.s094-s40: Because of me enmity will become manifest; the inner head of the Ahrimanic man will seethe.

sentences.s094-s44: I'll go off to Amol and will not come to the battle; I will exchange my renown for disgrace.
sentences.s094-s17: It is not fitting, in a king's vengeance, that the eye be full of water and the heart full of wrath.

sentences.s094-s25: We have all been born of the mother, Death; we have bowed and given our necks to her.

sentences.s094-s59: Farokhandeh Zal cried 'Bravo, go!' — may you be joyful as long as moon and year endure.
sentences.s094-s60: A force of quarrelsome troops from the regiments of Zabul turned their face toward Amol.
